title: clearvars
summary: Clear variables from the active workspace, with optional exclusions.

gpu_support.notes: Runs on the host CPU because it mutates the session workspace rather than array contents.

description: `clearvars` removes variables from the active RunMat workspace. RunMat supports MATLAB-style command forms such as `clearvars x y` and exclusion forms such as `clearvars -except keep`, updating the session snapshot so hosts can drop cleared bindings.
behaviors: `clearvars` with no inputs clears all workspace variables created in the current session.
`clearvars x` removes the variable `x` while leaving other workspace bindings intact.
Multiple names can be supplied as separate inputs or command-form tokens, for example `clearvars x y z`.
`clearvars -except x y` removes all active workspace variables except `x` and `y`.
Function-call forms accept variable names as string scalars, row character vectors, or string arrays.
Clearing a name that does not exist is a no-op.
`clearvars` is a sink builtin and does not produce a meaningful output value.

examples.output: % x and y are removed from the workspace
% a and b are removed; c remains defined

examples.description: Keep selected variables while clearing the rest
examples.output: % answer remains defined; raw and tmp are removed

faqs.question: How is `clearvars` different from `clear`?
faqs.answer: `clearvars` focuses on workspace variables and supports the `-except` option for preserving named bindings while clearing the rest.

faqs.question: Can I clear only one variable?
faqs.answer: Yes. Use `clearvars x` or `clearvars("x")` to remove one variable, and pass multiple names to clear more than one binding in a single call.

faqs.question: Does `clearvars -except x` clear figures or the console?
faqs.answer: No. It only mutates workspace variables. Use `close all` for figures and `clc` for the visible console.

faqs.question: Does GPU residency matter?
faqs.answer: No. `clearvars` operates on session bookkeeping, not on array kernels or provider-managed computations.

gpu_behavior: `clearvars` performs host-side workspace mutation only. It does not launch GPU kernels, does not participate in fusion, and does not depend on acceleration providers.
